claims.claim_text: The opening war-story heading gives a dated, company-stage, and spend-specific anecdote.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: **Q1 2024. A Series C startup. $8 million annual cloud spend.**
claims.suggested_rewrite: A fast-growing company with a large cloud bill.
claims.rationale: This is a specific, unattributed war story with date, company stage, and dollar figure but no citable source.

claims.claim_text: The CFO/all-hands anecdote includes specific growth rates, billing-line-item counts, and a named workload cost question.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: The CFO called an all-hands meeting. Cloud costs had grown 340% year-over-year while revenue grew 180%. The engineering team had no visibility into which teams, services, or features drove the cost. The finance team's cloud bill showed 12,000 line items per month. When the VP of Engineering was asked "how much does the recommendation engine cost?", the honest answer was "we have no idea."
claims.suggested_rewrite: Leadership realized cloud spend was growing faster than expected, but the organization still lacked workload-level cost visibility and could not answer basic ownership questions from provider billing alone.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is fine, but the precise percentages, counts, roles, and quotes are unsourced.

claims.claim_text: The forensic-analysis anecdote gives exact underutilization, orphaned-resource counts, duration, and cross-AZ transfer spend.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: Three months of forensic analysis revealed: 38% of EC2 instances were running at under 10% CPU utilization. The company was paying on-demand prices for workloads that ran 24/7 (perfect candidates for reserved instances or savings plans). Twenty-six EBS volumes were orphaned -- detached from any instance but still accruing charges. A development EKS cluster that was "temporary" had been running for 14 months. And the biggest surprise: cross-AZ data transfer for their Kubernetes pods cost $14,000 per month -- a line item nobody had ever noticed because it was buried in the EC2 data transfer category.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Detailed cost reviews often uncover underutilized compute, steady workloads still billed at on-demand rates, orphaned storage, long-lived temporary environments, and overlooked network-transfer charges.
claims.rationale: This is an unsourced incident-style anecdote with false precision across multiple metrics.

claims.claim_text: The module claims a measured post-optimization reduction of 42% or $3.36 million annually without code changes.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: After implementing the techniques in this module -- right-sizing, committed use discounts, Kubecost for allocation, VPA for resource optimization, and spot instances for non-critical workloads -- they reduced cloud spend by 42% ($3.36 million annually) without changing a single line of application code.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Applying right-sizing, commitment discounts, workload-level cost allocation, and carefully chosen interruptible capacity can materially reduce cloud spend without requiring application rewrites.
claims.rationale: This outcome is presented as a real historical result but has no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: OpenCost became a CNCF Sandbox project in 2022 and reached Incubation in 2024.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? item 4
claims.rationale: The CNCF project page directly states the acceptance and incubation dates.

claims.claim_text: OpenCost was originally developed and open sourced by Kubecost and is free/open source.
claims.span_hint: section: OpenCost: The Open-Source Alternative; section: Did You Know? item 4
claims.rationale: The OpenCost repository states both its Kubecost origin and that it is a free, open-source distribution.

claims.claim_text: The module claims OpenCost's cost allocation API is integrated into several commercial FinOps platforms.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? item 4
claims.anchor_text: OpenCost's cost allocation API is now integrated into several commercial FinOps platforms.
claims.suggested_rewrite: OpenCost's APIs and data model are increasingly used across the Kubernetes cost-management ecosystem.
claims.rationale: The exact breadth of commercial FinOps-platform integration is not substantiated here by an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: The VPA note claims Auto mode aggressively scales down CPU requests and causes severe throttling on sudden traffic spikes.
claims.span_hint: section: Pillar 2 > A Note on VPA Auto-Update
claims.anchor_text: For workloads with unpredictable or bursty traffic, do not use VPA in `Auto` mode. It will aggressively scale down CPU requests during quiet periods, which causes severe CPU throttling when traffic suddenly spikes. Instead, use VPA in recommendation mode to find a safe baseline, and use HPA to scale out horizontally when load increases.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For bursty or unpredictable workloads, review VPA recommendations carefully before applying them automatically, and pair right-sizing with HPA when you need elastic horizontal scaling.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes docs support how VPA updates requests, but this sentence overstates a specific failure mode without a primary source that directly backs it.

claims.claim_text: AWS Budgets can run actions automatically when a budget threshold is exceeded.
claims.span_hint: section: Automated Budget Alerting and Anomaly Detection > Cloud Provider Anomaly Remediation
claims.rationale: AWS Budgets documentation explicitly describes automatic budget actions at thresholds.

claims.claim_text: Google Cloud budgets can automate cost control responses through programmatic notifications.
claims.span_hint: section: Automated Budget Alerting and Anomaly Detection > Cloud Provider Anomaly Remediation
claims.rationale: Google Cloud Billing documentation directly says budgets can automate cost control responses using programmatic notifications.

claims.claim_text: The module states interruptible capacity across AWS, GCP, and Azure offers 60-90% discounts.
claims.span_hint: section: Pillar 4: Spot Instance Lifecycle
claims.anchor_text: Spot instances (AWS) / Preemptible VMs (GCP) / Spot VMs (Azure) offer 60-90% discounts but can be interrupted with short notice. Kubernetes makes them practical by handling rescheduling automatically.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Interruptible capacity on the major clouds can be substantially cheaper than on-demand pricing, but the exact discount and interruption behavior vary by provider, region, and instance type.
claims.rationale: The cross-provider discount range is too specific for a single authoritative citation and varies over time.

claims.claim_text: The Spot golden-rules paragraph assumes a universal 2-minute warning and 120-second shutdown window across cloud providers.
claims.span_hint: section: Pillar 4: Spot Instance Lifecycle
claims.anchor_text: **Spot Instance Golden Rules:** Because cloud providers only give a 2-minute warning before reclaiming a Spot node, they are entirely unsuitable for legacy monoliths, stateful applications relying on local disk, or single-replica deployments. Spot instances should only run stateless, fault-tolerant workloads that can gracefully shut down within 120 seconds and have multiple replicas distributed across nodes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Spot Instance Golden Rules:** Because eviction notice windows are short and provider-specific, use Spot only for workloads that tolerate interruption, recover cleanly on other nodes, and do not depend on a single local-stateful replica.
claims.rationale: AWS uses a two-minute notice, but GCP and Azure documentation describe shorter provider-specific windows, so the universal 120-second claim is inaccurate.

claims.claim_text: AWS Node Termination Handler detects Spot interruption notices and drains nodes.
claims.span_hint: section: Pillar 4 > Spot Interruption Handling
claims.rationale: The upstream README directly documents Spot interruption detection and cordon/drain behavior.

claims.claim_text: The table assigns a typical monthly dollar range to unattached EBS volumes.
claims.span_hint: section: Orphaned Resource Cleanup > Common Orphaned Resources (row: Unattached EBS volumes)
claims.anchor_text: | Unattached EBS volumes | PVC deleted, PV not reclaimed | $8-$80 per volume |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Unattached EBS volumes | PVC deleted, PV not reclaimed | Recurring storage charges that scale with size and volume type |
claims.rationale: The exact monthly amount depends on volume size, type, and region, but the underlying lesson that unattached volumes keep costing money is well-supported.

claims.claim_text: The table assigns a fixed monthly price to unused Elastic IPs.
claims.span_hint: section: Orphaned Resource Cleanup > Common Orphaned Resources (row: Unused Elastic IPs)
claims.anchor_text: | Unused Elastic IPs | Service deleted, EIP not released | $3.65 each |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Unused Elastic IPs | Service deleted, EIP not released | Ongoing hourly public IPv4 charges until released |
claims.rationale: The precise monthly total depends on billing hours, but AWS pricing clearly supports the general cost principle.

claims.claim_text: The table assigns a fixed per-GB price to old EBS snapshots.
claims.span_hint: section: Orphaned Resource Cleanup > Common Orphaned Resources (row: Old EBS snapshots)
claims.anchor_text: | Old EBS snapshots | Backup policy with no expiry | $0.05/GB |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Old EBS snapshots | Backup policy with no expiry | Recurring snapshot storage charges until deleted or archived |
claims.rationale: Snapshot pricing varies by storage class and context, but the storage-cost principle is directly documented.

claims.claim_text: The table assigns a typical monthly dollar range to idle load balancers.
claims.span_hint: section: Orphaned Resource Cleanup > Common Orphaned Resources (row: Idle load balancers)
claims.anchor_text: | Idle load balancers | Service deleted, LB remains | $16-$25 each |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Idle load balancers | Service deleted, LB remains | Ongoing hourly and usage-based charges until deleted |
claims.rationale: Load balancer costs depend on load-balancer type and usage dimensions, so a generic pricing lesson is safer than a hard number.

claims.claim_text: The table assigns a fixed monthly price to orphaned NAT Gateways.
claims.span_hint: section: Orphaned Resource Cleanup > Common Orphaned Resources (row: Orphaned NAT Gateways)
claims.anchor_text: | Orphaned NAT Gateways | VPC deleted, NAT GW remains | $32 each |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Orphaned NAT Gateways | VPC deleted, NAT GW remains | Ongoing hourly and data-processing charges until deleted |
claims.rationale: The concrete monthly total depends on region and hours, but AWS pricing directly supports the ongoing-cost principle.

claims.claim_text: The table assigns a fixed per-GB price to unused RDS snapshots.
claims.span_hint: section: Orphaned Resource Cleanup > Common Orphaned Resources (row: Unused RDS snapshots)
claims.anchor_text: | Unused RDS snapshots | Manual snapshots accumulated | $0.095/GB |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Unused RDS snapshots | Manual snapshots accumulated | Recurring snapshot storage charges until deleted |
claims.rationale: The exact rate is region- and product-dependent, but AWS RDS docs support the persistent-cost lesson for retained snapshots.

claims.claim_text: The module claims typical Kubernetes clusters run at 30-50% utilization and waste 50-70% of compute spend based on Kubecost data across thousands of clusters.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? item 1
claims.anchor_text: **Kubernetes clusters typically run at 30-50% resource utilization** according to data from Kubecost across thousands of clusters. This means 50-70% of compute spend is wasted on idle resources. The primary cause is over-provisioned resource requests: developers set CPU and memory requests based on worst-case scenarios and never revisit them. VPA in recommendation mode can identify right-sizing opportunities without any risk.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Many Kubernetes environments run below their provisioned capacity.** Over-provisioned resource requests are a common source of waste, and recommendation tooling can help identify safer right-sizing opportunities before you change live workloads.
claims.rationale: This is a precise utilization/waste statistic tied to a named dataset, but no allowlisted primary source was located for the exact numbers.

claims.claim_text: The Spot-interruption-rate paragraph gives dynamic, region-specific example percentages for AWS Spot instance types.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? item 2
claims.anchor_text: **AWS Spot instances have been interrupted less than 5% of the time** for the most popular instance types (m5.xlarge, m6i.xlarge) in US regions, based on the AWS Spot Instance Advisor. The interruption rate varies dramatically by instance type and region: r5.8xlarge in ap-southeast-1 might see 15-20% interruption rate, while m7i.xlarge in us-east-1 sees under 3%. Diversifying across instance types and AZs is the key to reliable Spot usage.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **AWS publishes rolling interruption-frequency data for Spot capacity.** Interruption rates vary by instance type and region, so check current Spot Advisor data and diversify across instance pools and Availability Zones instead of relying on fixed example percentages.
claims.rationale: The exact example rates are time-sensitive and not stably citable as written.

claims.claim_text: The cross-AZ traffic paragraph claims this is a top-5 AWS Kubernetes cost category, gives a monthly estimate, and says GCP made cross-zone traffic free in 2022.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? item 3
claims.anchor_text: **Cross-AZ data transfer is one of the top 5 cost categories** for most Kubernetes deployments on AWS. A company running 20 microservices with 100 pods across 3 AZs can easily spend $2,000-$5,000/month on cross-AZ traffic alone. GCP made cross-zone traffic free in 2022. AWS has not followed suit, making topology-aware routing a significant cost optimization lever for AWS-based Kubernetes deployments.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Cross-zone or cross-AZ network traffic can become a meaningful Kubernetes cost driver.** Provider pricing differs, so verify your platform's current network charges before treating topology-aware routing as a cost-optimization lever.
claims.rationale: This paragraph mixes an unsupported ranking claim, an illustrative monthly estimate, and an inaccurate statement about Google Cloud cross-zone pricing.

claims.claim_text: The Savings Plans row recommends committing to 60-70% of average usage.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes (row: Buying Savings Plans based on current usage)
claims.anchor_text: | Buying Savings Plans based on current usage | "We're using $10K/month now, commit to $10K" | Usage fluctuates. Commit to 60-70% of your average usage. The rest stays on-demand for flexibility. Over-commitment is worse than no commitment. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Buying Savings Plans based on current usage | "We're using $10K/month now, commit to $10K" | Usage fluctuates. Commit conservatively to stable baseline usage and validate it with provider recommendation tools before buying long-term commitments. |
claims.rationale: The exact 60-70% rule is a heuristic, but AWS does provide recommendation tooling that supports the general lesson.

claims.claim_text: The dev/staging row claims a $3,000/month cluster running only business hours would cost $900.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes (row: Running dev/staging clusters 24/7)
claims.anchor_text: | Running dev/staging clusters 24/7 | "Someone might need them on weekends" | Implement auto-shutdown for non-production clusters. Scale to zero outside business hours. A $3,000/month staging cluster running only business hours costs $900. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Running dev/staging clusters 24/7 | "Someone might need them on weekends" | Implement auto-shutdown for non-production clusters and scale down idle capacity outside working hours to cut spend substantially. |
claims.rationale: The exact monthly outcome is illustrative, but pay-as-you-go compute pricing supports the broader shutdown-for-savings lesson.

claims.claim_text: The Spot-diversification row recommends exactly 4-6 compatible instance types and says Karpenter handles this automatically.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes (row: Not diversifying Spot instance types)
claims.anchor_text: | Not diversifying Spot instance types | "We need m7i.xlarge specifically" | Spot pools with a single instance type have higher interruption rates. Specify 4-6 compatible instance types. Karpenter handles this automatically. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Not diversifying Spot instance types | "We need m7i.xlarge specifically" | Single-type Spot pools are more fragile. Use multiple compatible instance types, and let provisioning tools such as Karpenter manage that diversity where appropriate. |
claims.rationale: The exact 4-6 number is heuristic, but AWS guidance clearly supports diversifying Spot pools and using Karpenter to help manage them.

further_reading.title: OpenCost CNCF Project
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary project overview for OpenCost's scope, governance, and maturity level.

further_reading.title: Topology Aware Routing
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains how Kubernetes prefers same-zone traffic, which is relevant when network-transfer pricing matters.

further_reading.title: AWS Savings Plans Purchase Recommendations
further_reading.why_relevant: Shows how to derive commitment levels from measured usage instead of fixed rule-of-thumb percentages.

notes: Mermaid diagrams, code blocks, quiz prompts/answers, and hands-on exercise solutions were intentionally excluded per pipeline rules. `source_ids` are empty because no shared section pool was selected; claim-local URLs are attached via `proposed_url` where applicable.
